title: Terrorist Finance Tracking Program (TFTP)
summary: The U.S. Department of the Treasury provides an overview of the Terrorist Finance Tracking Program (TFTP), a counter-terrorism initiative that uses subpoenas to SWIFT to obtain financial messaging data for identifying and disrupting terrorist networks. The program operates under legal authorities including IEEPA and UNPA, with an agreement with the European Union effective August 1, 2010, governing data processing and transfers.
key_points: TFTP was initiated after September 11, 2001 to identify, track, and pursue terrorists including Al-Qaeda, Hizballah, and HAMAS
Treasury issues subpoenas to SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) for financial transaction data related to suspected terrorism
Data storage moved from U.S. servers to the European Union at end of 2009, with U.S.-EU agreement effective August 1, 2010
Program operates under legal authority including International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and United Nations Participation Act (UNPA)
TFTP explicitly states it does not involve data mining and maintains privacy safeguards including systematic logging and independent audits
SWIFT is overseen by a committee of major central banks including Federal Reserve, Bank of England, ECB, Bank of Japan, and National Bank of Belgium
